step.title: Step 3
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The maze has a gray room (G) with charcoal walls (c). Player (white cross) is at left side. Orange target (O) is at bottom-right. The path goes DOWN through the left corridor then RIGHT at row 45 where the wall opens up.
- Goal model: Navigate to the orange target area.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player character.
- Plan: Go DOWN 12 more steps to row 45, then RIGHT 13 steps to the orange target.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player is STILL at the same position: pixels at [(31, 21), (32, 20), (32, 21), (32, 22), (33, 21)]. This means the 8 DOWN actions didn't move the player at all! The player center is still at (32, 21).
This is strange. The first DOWN action moved the player from (31, 21) to (32, 21), but then the 8 DOWN actions in the batch didn't move the player further. 
Wait - looking at the result more carefully: it says `no_impact: True` and `stop_reason: 'no_impact_action'`. This means the 9th action (which was the first action checked for no_impact) had no impact, and the batch stopped. But it also says `executed_count: 8`. 
Hmm, but the player is still at the same position as after the first DOWN. This suggests that maybe the 8 DOWN actions in the batch were all no_impact too, but the system only reported the first no_impact one that stopped the batch.
Or wait - maybe the player DID move, but then something else happened. Let me check the transitions more carefully.
Actually, looking at the player pixels: [(31, 21), (32, 20), (32, 21), (32, 22), (33, 21)]. The center W pixel is at (32, 21). This is the same position as after the first DOWN action. So the 8 DOWN actions in the batch didn't move the player.
This is very strange. Let me check if maybe the player hit a wall immediately. Looking at the maze layout:
- Row 33, col 21: G (walkable)
- Row 34, col 21: G (walkable)
So the player should be able to move DOWN. Unless... the player's cross shape is colliding with something.
Wait, I think I need to reconsider. The player is a cross shape with center at (32, 21) and arms at (31, 21), (33, 21), (32, 20), (32, 22). When the player tries to move DOWN, the new position would have center at (33, 21) and arms at (32, 21), (34, 21), (33, 20), (33, 22).
Looking at the maze:
- (33, 20): G
- (33, 22): G
- (34, 21): G
All walkable. So why didn't the player move?
Oh wait - maybe the issue is that the batch was stopped because the 9th action had no impact, but the first 8 actions were executed. Let me check the transitions to see what actually happened.
Actually, I realize the issue might be simpler. The `no_impact: True` in the result means the LAST executed action had no impact. But the batch executed 8 actions before stopping. So maybe the player moved 8 times, but then the 9th action (which would have been the 9th DOWN) had no impact.
But the player position shows the center at (32, 21), which is only 1 DOWN from the starting position. So the 8 DOWN actions in the batch didn't move the player at all.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me try a single DOWN action to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is still at (32, 21) - the 8 DOWNs didn't move the player
# Let me check transitions to understand what happened
print(f"Number of transitions: {len(transitions)}")
for i, t in enumerate(transitions):
    print(f"Transition {i}: action={t.action}, board_changed={t.result.get('board_changed')}, no_impact={t.result.get('no_impact')}")

# Try a single DOWN to see what happens
result = action(['DOWN'])
print(f"\nSingle DOWN result: {result}")

# Check player position after
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
player_positions = []
for r in range(len(ascii_lines)):
    for c in range(len(ascii_lines[r])):
        if ascii_lines[r][c] in ('W', 'w'):
            player_positions.append((r, c))
print(f"Player pixels after single DOWN: {player_positions}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
